does: This paper introduces COMPASS, a test-time reinforcement learning reward mechanism that operates without human-labeled data by combining two components: Dual-Calibration Answer Reward (DCAR) for establishing reliable pseudo-labels through confidence calibration, and a path-based reward signal that scores the reasoning trajectory itself rather than only final answers. The method enables LLMs to learn from continuous unlabeled data streams through autonomous experience, addressing the scalability bottleneck of existing RL approaches that depend heavily on curated preference data or labeled datasets for reward modeling.
connects: This work extends recent test-time RL methods for LLMs that leverage self-consistent consensus voting, building on approaches like those in mathematical reasoning and code generation. It responds to limitations of majority-voting based pseudo-labeling that can reinforce incorrect consensus answers, and connects to broader literature on unsupervised reward learning and self-supervised RL. The integration of confidence calibration techniques and trajectory-level rewards builds on prior work exploring how intermediate steps contribute to final performance in complex reasoning tasks, rather than treating reasoning as a black-box outcome.
enables: This approach opens pathways toward scaling RL training of LLMs without human annotation bottlenecks, potentially enabling continuous learning from unlabeled problem instances. The composite scoring mechanism combining answer confidence with path quality could extend to other domains requiring multi-step reasoning beyond mathematics and code. The work points toward fully autonomous RL systems that improve through interaction with problems rather than curated feedback, potentially enabling more efficient adaptation to domain-specific tasks and real-time learning in deployment settings where labeled data is unavailable or expensive.
